text: The ABCDE of melanoma detection is a useful guide when checking your skin. A is for Asymmetry: look for spots that lack symmetry, where if a line was drawn through the middle, the two sides would not match up. B is for Border: a spot with a spreading or irregular, notched edge. C is for Colour: blotchy spots with a number of colours such as black, blue, red, white and/or grey. D is for Diameter: look for spots that are getting bigger. E is for Evolving: spots that are changing and growing. Other changes to look out for include new moles, moles that increase in size, an outline that becomes notched, a spot that changes colour from brown to black or becomes varied, a spot that becomes raised or develops a lump, a mole surface becoming rough or scaly, moles that itch, tingle, bleed or weep.
source_title: Check for signs of skin cancer | Cancer Council Australia

text: It's important to get to know your skin and what is normal for you, so that you notice any changes. Skin cancers rarely hurt and are much more frequently seen than felt. Develop a regular habit of checking your skin for new spots and changes to existing freckles or moles. To check your skin: undress completely and make sure you have good light; use a mirror to check hard-to-see spots like your back and scalp, or get a family member, partner or friend to check for you; and make sure you check your entire body, as skin cancers can sometimes occur on parts not exposed to the sun, such as the soles of the feet, between fingers and toes, and under nails.
source_title: Check for signs of skin cancer | Cancer Council Australia

text: To make sure you don't miss anything when checking your skin: stand in front of a full-length mirror in a well-lit room and start at the top, working your way down your body. Part your hair into sections with a brush or hairdryer to check your scalp. Move to your face and neck, including your ears, nostrils and lips. Check both the top and underneath of your arms, and don't forget your fingernails. Check places that may never have seen much sun, since melanoma can be found on skin without sun exposure. Ask a partner or family member to check your scalp and back. The best way to monitor changes is by taking photographs every few months and comparing them to identify any changes, and reacting quickly if something is growing or changing.
source_title: Checking Your Skin | Melanoma Institute Australia

text: Melanoma is the most deadly form of skin cancer. If left untreated, it can spread to other parts of the body. It appears as a new spot or an existing spot that changes in colour, size or shape, and can appear on skin not normally exposed to the sun. Nodular melanoma is a distinct subtype: it grows quickly and looks different from common melanomas. It is raised and even in colour; many are red or pink, some brown or black; they are firm to touch and dome-shaped; and after a while they begin to bleed and crust. Remember that melanomas are not always black — they can also be brown, pink, skin-coloured, or a combination of different shades.
source_title: Check for signs of skin cancer | Cancer Council Australia

text: Basal cell carcinoma (BCC) is the most common and least dangerous form of skin cancer, accounting for about 70% of non-melanoma skin cancers. It begins in the lower layer of the epidermis and can appear anywhere on the body, but most commonly develops on parts of the body that receive high or intermittent sun exposure, such as the head, face, neck, shoulders and back. BCC often has no symptoms and tends to grow slowly without spreading to other parts of the body. Symptoms can include a pearly lump, or a scaly, dry area that is shiny and pale or bright pink in colour. It may ulcerate or fail to completely heal.
source_title: Basal and Squamous Cell Carcinoma | Non-melanoma Skin Cancer | Cancer Council Australia

text: Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) accounts for about 30% of non-melanoma skin cancers. It begins in the upper layer of the epidermis and usually appears where the skin has had the most exposure to the sun, such as the head, neck, hands, forearms and lower legs. SCC generally grows quickly over weeks or months. Symptoms may include a thickened, red, scaly spot, a rapidly growing lump, a sore that has not healed, and tenderness to touch. While SCC is a less serious form of skin cancer than melanoma, it can grow quickly and spread, causing potentially serious complications if untreated.
source_title: Basal and Squamous Cell Carcinoma | Non-melanoma Skin Cancer | Cancer Council Australia

text: Non-melanoma skin cancers, now called keratinocyte cancers, are the most common cancers in Australia, though most are not life-threatening. Basal cell carcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma together make up about 99% of all skin cancers. Between 95% and 99% of skin cancers in Australia are caused by exposure to the sun. Australia has one of the highest skin cancer rates in the world, and Cancer Council Australia forecasts that at least two out of three Australians will be diagnosed with a skin cancer in their lifetime. The majority of basal cell and squamous cell carcinomas are successfully treated, and skin cancer is visible, mostly preventable, and curable when detected and treated early.
source_title: Basal and Squamous Cell Carcinoma | Non-melanoma Skin Cancer | Cancer Council Australia

text: Almost all of us have moles. Moles are not normally present at birth, but appear in childhood and early teenage years — by the age of 15, Australian children have an average of more than 50 moles. Normal moles usually look alike; they are harmless coloured spots ranging from 1mm to 10mm, uniform in shape and even coloured, and may be raised. See your doctor if a mole looks different, or if a new mole appears after the age of 25. The more moles or freckles a person has, the higher their risk of skin cancer. Observing one or more skin changes does not necessarily mean skin cancer, but it is important to have it investigated by a GP. When in doubt, speak to your GP.
source_title: Check for signs of skin cancer | Cancer Council Australia

text: Skin types that are more sensitive to ultraviolet (UV) radiation burn more quickly and are at greater risk of skin cancer, though all skin types can be damaged by too much UV. Type I often burns and rarely tans, with freckles, red or fair hair, and blue or green eyes. Type II usually burns and sometimes tans, with light hair and blue or brown eyes. Type III sometimes burns and usually tans, with brown hair and eyes. Type IV rarely burns and often tans, with dark brown eyes and hair. Type V has dark brown skin, rarely burns, and tans profusely. Type VI is deeply pigmented, dark brown to black skin, and never burns. People with naturally very dark skin still need to take care in the sun even though they may rarely, if ever, get sunburnt — the larger amount of melanin provides natural protection, lowering skin cancer risk, but vitamin D deficiency may be a greater health concern for this skin type since it is more difficult to make vitamin D.
source_title: Check for signs of skin cancer | Cancer Council Australia

text: Ultraviolet (UV) radiation is an invisible form of energy produced by the sun that can be high even on cool and overcast days, so you can't rely on clear skies or high temperatures to know when to protect yourself. There are three types of UV radiation by wavelength: UVA can cause sunburn, DNA damage in the skin, and skin cancer; UVB causes skin damage and skin cancer, with ozone stopping most of it (about 15% is transmitted); UVC is the most dangerous type, but ozone in the atmosphere absorbs all of it and it does not reach the earth's surface. Exposure to UV radiation from the sun and other sources such as solariums is the major cause of skin cancer. Just 15 minutes of unprotected exposure, when UV levels are 3 or above, is enough to start damaging skin.
source_title: UV Index | Cancer Council Australia

text: The UV Index is a tool used to protect against UV radiation, telling you the times of day you need to be SunSmart. It divides UV radiation levels into: low (1-2), moderate (3-5), high (6-7), very high (8-10), and extreme (11 and above). Sun protection times are issued by the Bureau of Meteorology when the UV Index is forecast to reach 3 or above, since at that level it can damage skin and lead to skin cancer. UV levels are affected by geographic location, altitude, time of day, time of year and cloud cover, meaning UV levels can be higher in some parts of Australia than others on the same day. The Index is available on the Bureau of Meteorology website, in newspaper weather pages, and via the SunSmart app.
source_title: UV Index | Cancer Council Australia

text: When the UV Index is 3 or above, follow the five SunSmart steps: slip on protective clothing to cover your skin, choosing long-sleeve tops and pants for better coverage; slop on SPF 50 or higher broad-spectrum, water-resistant sunscreen and reapply every two hours, especially after swimming, sweating or towel-drying; slap on a broad-brimmed hat to protect your face, neck and ears, with a brim of at least 7.5cm; seek shade, especially during peak UV hours, usually between 10am and 4pm; and slide on sunglasses that meet Australian standards to protect your eyes from UV damage. Even on cool, cloudy days the UV can still be strong, so always check the UV Index and follow these steps whenever the UV level is 3 or higher.
source_title: UV Index | Cancer Council Australia

text: Using SPF 50+ broad-spectrum sunscreen is one of the 5 Sun Safe Rules to minimise skin cancer risk, including melanoma. In Australia, all sunscreens are regulated by the Therapeutic Goods Administration. Most Australians don't apply enough sunscreen — a good guide is the '7 teaspoon' rule: one teaspoon for each limb, one teaspoon each for the front and back of the torso, and half a teaspoon each for the face and neck. Sunscreen should be reapplied about every two hours, or more frequently if swimming or exercising. SPF is the Sun Protection Factor against UVB, which causes most damage; for example, SPF 50 provides 50 times the amount of time without sun damage compared to using no sunscreen at all.
source_title: How to Prevent Melanoma | Melanoma Institute Australia

text: There is no safe way to tan. A tan is actually skin cells in trauma — the result of sun-damaged cells producing melanin in an attempt to protect themselves from further UV damage, a form of the body's self-preservation response, not a sign of health. The main risk factor for developing melanoma is UV over-exposure, and sunburn during childhood, particularly before puberty, greatly increases the risk of developing melanoma as an adult. Commercial sunbeds (solariums) are illegal in Australia because they greatly increase the risk of developing melanoma.
source_title: How to Prevent Melanoma | Melanoma Institute Australia

text: UV radiation from the sun is one of the main causes of melanoma, but it is also one of the best natural sources of vitamin D, so a balance is important. For most people, adequate vitamin D levels are reached through regular incidental sun exposure, such as walking to the bus or hanging out washing, and using sensible sun protection when outdoors does not usually put a person at risk of vitamin D deficiency. Vitamin D is essential for musculoskeletal health but can be supplemented if needed — anyone concerned about their vitamin D status should see their GP to have it checked and treated accordingly, rather than deliberately increasing unprotected sun exposure.
source_title: How to Prevent Melanoma | Melanoma Institute Australia

text: Your risk of skin cancer increases as you get older. You may also be at higher risk if you have had skin cancer before, or a family member has had it; have had bad sunburn in the past; have fair skin, light-coloured eyes, or red or fair hair; have many moles on your skin; spend a lot of time outdoors without sun protection or work outdoors; have used solariums or sun lamps; or have a weakened immune system. Skin cancer may happen when skin cells are damaged by ultraviolet (UV) radiation from the sun.
source_title: Should I be checked for skin cancer? | healthdirect

text: Check your skin regularly and see your doctor if you notice any new spots or changes to your moles. If you are at a high risk of skin cancer, see your doctor for a full skin check every 6 months, and check your own skin every 3 months. You can use an online risk calculator to check your risk of melanoma. Most melanomas are found by individuals themselves, or by their partners or other family members, which is why knowing your own skin well matters. During a skin cancer check, a doctor may ask about your moles, risk factors and medical history, examine your skin all over your body including scalp, palms, soles, nails and inside your mouth, and use a magnifying tool called a dermatoscope to closely examine any suspicious spots.
source_title: Should I be checked for skin cancer? | healthdirect

text: You are not advised to use a smartphone app to check for skin cancer. These apps are not accurate. Several smartphone apps allow you to photograph your skin and set reminders for skin checks, and can be helpful for keeping track of moles over time to make it easier to detect changes — however, they cannot reliably diagnose skin cancer and should not replace a visit to the doctor if you find something you are concerned about. Speak to your doctor about checking your skin for skin cancer and let them know about any spots or changes that are worrying you.
source_title: Should I be checked for skin cancer? | healthdirect

text: It is important to get a professional skin check by a GP or dermatologist if anything suspicious appears on your skin, or if you are high risk. Doctors use tools and techniques to examine skin thoroughly, beyond what the naked eye can see, including dermoscopy using a dermatoscope, digital monitoring, and total body photography. These tools help reduce unnecessary biopsies and can detect melanomas at an early stage. A GP can perform a skin check, examine lesions of concern, discuss risk factors and family history, treat some skin cancers, and refer to a dermatologist if needed. For specialist advice or high-risk patients, a referral to a dermatologist — a doctor with additional training in diagnosing and treating skin disease including skin cancers — may be recommended.
source_title: Checking Your Skin | Melanoma Institute Australia

text: See your doctor or dermatologist straight away if you notice any changes to your skin, such as: crusty sores that don't heal; changes to the colour, size, shape or thickness of moles and freckles over a few weeks or months; new spots on your skin; or small lumps that are red, pale or pearly in colour. It is important to know your skin well so you can notice changes early — most melanomas are found by individuals or by their partners or other family members. Early detection is important because it improves treatment outcomes and increases the chances of successful treatment.
source_title: Should I be checked for skin cancer? | healthdirect
